title: Arithmetic Symbols Group
definition: Basic arithmetic symbols: plus (+), minus (-), asterisk (*), slash (/), percent (%). Used for math operations in both math and code.
image_url: Remember: In coding, * is always multiplication, not ·

title: Relational (Comparison) Symbols Group
definition: Symbols that compare two values and return true/false: = (assignment), == (equal to), != (not equal to), < (less), > (greater), <=, >=.
image_url: In code, = is assignment, == is comparison. Don't mix them!

title: Logical (Boolean) Symbols Group
definition: Logical operators: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT). Also bitwise: &, |, ^, ~. Used for conditions and bit manipulation.
image_url: && and || short-circuit: they stop early if first condition decides outcome.

title: Assignment & Increment/Decrement Group
definition: Assignment operators: =, +=, -=, ++, --. They change variable values. ++ adds 1, -- subtracts 1.
image_url: ++x increments first, then returns; x++ returns then increments.

title: Grouping & Block Symbols Group
definition: Symbols that group code or expressions: parentheses (), square brackets [], curly braces {}, angle brackets <>.
image_url: Parentheses change math order: (a+b)*c is not a+b*c.

title: Backslash Symbol Group
definition: Backslash \ is rare in math (set difference: A \ B) but very common in code: escape sequences (\n newline, \t tab), Windows paths (C:\Users), and line continuation.
image_url: Never confuse \ (backslash) with / (forward slash). Say "backslash" clearly.

title: Constants & Set Symbols Group
definition: Special math symbols used in code as constants or in comments: π (pi), e (Euler's number), ∞ (infinity), √ (square root), ∈ (belongs to), ∪ (union), ∩ (intersection).
description: In code, use Math.PI, Math.E, Math.sqrt(), and array methods for sets.

title: Punctuation & Special Symbols Group
definition: Symbols used almost exclusively in code, not math: double quote (), single quote ('), hyphen/dash (- in identifiers), underscore (_), at (@), hash (#), dollar ($), dot (.), comma (,), semicolon (
sentence: ), colon (:), question mark (?), ellipsis (...), backtick (`). Essential for reading code aloud."
description: Always say the symbol name exactly – never substitute descriptions like "that squiggly thing".

title: Plus sign (+)
definition: Addition operator. In math: sum of two numbers. In code: numeric addition or string concatenation.
image_url: In JavaScript, + with a string converts numbers to string.

title: Minus sign (-)
definition: Subtraction or negation. In math: difference. In code: subtract or make negative.
image_url: Use - for both subtraction and unary minus.

title: Asterisk (*)
definition: Multiplication operator. Also used as pointer dereference (C/C++) or wildcard (file search). In math, x*y means x times y.
image_url: Never use 'x' for multiplication in code – always *.

title: Slash (/)
definition: Division operator. In math: a/b = a divided by b. In code: also used as path separator (Linux/macOS). In some languages, integer division truncates.
image_url: For exact division, use float numbers: 7.0/2 = 3.5

title: Percent (%)
definition: Modulo operator: remainder after division. In math: 10 mod 3 = 1. Very common in programming for even/odd checks and cyclic values.
image_url: Modulo with negative numbers varies by language. In most, sign follows dividend.

title: Equals sign (=) and Double equals (==)
definition: = is assignment: sets a variable to a value. == is comparison: checks if two values are equal. Confusing them is a common bug.
example4: Math: = means equality in equations, but code uses == for that
image_url: In many languages, use === for strict equality (type + value).

title: Not Equal (!=)
definition: Inequality operator. Returns true if two values are different. In math, it's ≠, but in code we write !=.
image_url: Some languages use <> for not equal, but != is most common.

title: Less than (<) and Greater than (>)
definition: Compare numeric values. < means strictly smaller, > strictly larger. Also used as angle brackets in generics/HTML.
image_url: Be careful: in generics, <T> is not comparison.

title: Less than or equal (<=) and Greater than or equal (>=)
definition: Inclusive comparisons. <= means smaller or equal, >= means larger or equal.
usage1: Math: x ≥ 0 means x is zero or positive
image_url: Use these for ranges: if (x >= 10 && x <= 20)

title: Logical AND (&&) and OR (||)
definition: && returns true only if both sides true. || returns true if at least one side true. Short-circuit evaluation.
example4: Code: let result = a && b (if a false, b not evaluated)
image_url: Use && for safety: if (obj && obj.prop)

title: Logical NOT (!)
definition: Negation operator. Turns true to false and false to true. Placed before a boolean expression. Also called 'bang' in some contexts.
image_url: Double NOT (!!) converts any value to boolean.

title: Increment (++) and Decrement (--)
definition: Add 1 or subtract 1 to a variable. Can be prefix (++x) or postfix (x++). Difference: prefix returns new value, postfix returns old.
example2: Code: count++ (add 1 after using current value)
usage1: Code: let x = 5; let y = x++; // y=5, x=6
image_url: Use ++x in expressions when you need the updated value immediately.

title: Plus equals (+=) and Minus equals (-=)
definition: Compound assignment: x += y means x = x + y. Similarly -=, *=, /=, %=. Shorter and cleaner.
example2: Code: total += price (same as total = total + price)
image_url: These operators modify the variable in place.

title: Parentheses ()
definition: Group expressions to change evaluation order. Also used for function calls and control structures.
image_url: Always use parentheses to make order clear, even when not required.

title: Square brackets []
definition: Used for array indexing and list creation. Also in regular expressions and character classes.
image_url: Index usually starts at 0 (zero-based).

title: Curly braces {}
definition: Define code blocks, objects, and sets. In JavaScript/JSON, they create objects. In C-like languages, they group statements.
image_url: Every opening brace { must have a closing }.

title: Angle brackets <>
definition: Used for generics in languages like Java, C#, C++. Also for HTML tags. In math, they mean less than/greater than, but in generics they are not comparisons.
image_url: Angle brackets in generics are not less than – they are part of type syntax.

title: Backslash \
definition: Escape character in strings. Turns special characters into literal ones or creates control characters. Also Windows path separator.
image_url: To print a backslash, use double backslash: "\\"

title: Pi (π)
definition: Mathematical constant ~3.14159. Ratio of circumference to diameter. In code, use Math.PI (JavaScript) or math.pi (Python).

title: Square root (√)
definition: Returns a number that, when multiplied by itself, gives the original. In code, use sqrt() function.
image_url: Negative numbers give NaN (Not a Number) in most languages.

title: Set symbols (∈, ∪, ∩)
definition: ∈ means "belongs to" (element of a set). ∪ is union (combine sets). ∩ is intersection (common elements). Used in algorithms and comments.
example3: Math: A ∪ B = all elements in A or B
example4: Math: A ∩ B = elements in both
image_url: In code, use array methods like includes(), concat(), filter() for set operations.

title: Double quote (")
definition: Used to delimit string literals in most programming languages. Everything between two double quotes is treated as text.
image_url: Double quotes are often interchangeable with single quotes in JavaScript/Python, but not in C/Java.

title: Single quote (')
definition: Used for character literals in C/Java (single character) and for strings in some languages like Python/JavaScript. In many languages, single quotes cannot span multiple lines.
example4: Code: '' (empty character not allowed in C)
image_url: In JSON, only double quotes are allowed.

title: Hyphen (as dash) - in identifiers
definition: When used inside a name (e.g., CSS class `user-profile`), it is called hyphen or dash. It is NOT subtraction. Most programming languages do NOT allow hyphen in variable names because it would be interpreted as minus.
example4: File name: my-file.txt -> "my hyphen file dot txt"
usage1: In math: 5-3 -> here it is minus, not hyphen
image_url: If you see a hyphen inside an identifier, say 'hyphen', not 'minus'.

title: Underscore (_)
definition: Used in variable names to separate words (snake_case). Often indicates private or internal fields. Also used as a placeholder for ignored values.
image_url: Underscore is a letter as far as the compiler is concerned – you can use it anywhere.

title: At sign (@)
definition: Used for decorators in Python, annotations in Java, and email addresses. Also used in some languages for special string literals (C# verbatim strings).
usage1: Email: user@example.com -> "user at example dot com"
description: When reading email, say 'at', not 'at sign'.

title: Hash (#)
definition: Used for single-line comments in Python, Ruby, and many scripting languages. Also for preprocessor directives in C/C++ and ID selectors in CSS. Common names: 'hash', 'pound', 'octothorpe'.
example2: Python: # This is a comment -> "hash This is a comment"
image_url: Most programmers say 'hash' for # in code. 'Pound' is more common in US telephones.

title: Dollar sign ($)
definition: Used for variables in PHP, shell scripting, and jQuery. Also used in regex for end-of-line.
example3: jQuery: $('div') -> "dollar open parenthesis quote div quote close parenthesis"
image_url: In JavaScript template literals, `${var}` is called 'dollar curly brace'.

title: Dot (.), period
definition: Used for object property access, decimal point in numbers, and directory separator in some contexts. Also used in import statements.
image_url: Always say 'dot' for property access, not 'period'.

title: Comma (,)
definition: Separates arguments in function calls, elements in arrays, and items in variable declarations.
example2: Code: function add(a, b, c) -> "a comma b comma c"
example3: Code: let arr = [1, 2, 3]; -> "one comma two comma three"
example4: Code: for (i = 0, j = 10; i < j; i++, j--)
image_url: Pause slightly after each comma when reading aloud.

title: Semicolon (
question_text: A1
sentence: Terminates statements in C, Java, JavaScript, and many other languages. Optional in some (JavaScript automatic semicolon insertion, Python uses newlines).
image_url: In JavaScript: missing semicolon can cause bugs
audio_url: Read it as 'semicolon' – not 'end of line'.

title: Colon (:)
definition: Used in ternary operator (`? :`), label in JavaScript, dictionary key-value in Python, and inheritance in some languages (C++). Also in switch statements.
example2: Ternary: condition ? a : b -> "question mark a colon b"
usage1: C++ inheritance: class Dog : public Animal
image_url: Colon is also used in time (10:30) but that's not code.

title: Question mark (?)
definition: Used in ternary operator (`? :`), optional chaining (`?.` in JavaScript), and as a wildcard in some contexts. Also used in nullable types (C#).
example3: Ternary: condition ? trueValue : falseValue -> "question mark trueValue colon falseValue"
example4: Optional chaining: obj?.prop -> "obj question mark dot prop"

title: Ellipsis (...)
definition: Three dots in a row. Used as rest/spread operator in JavaScript, varargs in Java, and range in some languages. Also indicates omission in comments.
example3: JavaScript rest: function f(...args) -> "dot dot dot args"
usage1: Python range: for i in range(1, 10, 2) (not ellipsis)
image_url: Most common reading: 'dot dot dot' or 'ellipsis'.

title: Backtick (`)
definition: Used for template literals in JavaScript (allow embedded expressions) and for command substitution in shell scripts. Also used in Markdown for inline code.
example2: JavaScript: `Hello ${name}` -> "backtick Hello dollar curly brace name close brace backtick"
image_url: Never say 'single quote' for backtick – they are different.

title: Escape sequence: newline (\n)
definition: Represents a line break. When read aloud, say 'backslash n'. Used in strings to format output.
image_url: In most languages, \n is a single character (ASCII 10).

title: Escape sequence: tab (\t)
definition: Represents a horizontal tab character. Used for aligning text. Read as 'backslash t'.
example2: Code: print("Name\tAge\nJohn\t25") -> "Name backslash t Age backslash n John backslash t 25"
image_url: Tab width varies depending on environment (usually 2-8 spaces).

title: Escape sequence: carriage return (\r)
definition: Older line ending character (used with \n in Windows: \r\n). Read as 'backslash r'. Rarely used alone in modern code.
example2: Windows line endings: "Line1\r\nLine2" -> "Line1 backslash r backslash n Line2"
usage1: Not common in modern high-level languages
image_url: In JavaScript, \r is still valid but rarely needed.

title: Symbol identification: modulo
question_text: Which symbol gives the remainder after division?
explanation: % is the modulo operator. Example: 10 % 3 = 1.

title: Logical AND symbol
question_text: Which symbol represents logical AND in most programming languages?
explanation: && is logical AND. Example: if (x > 0 && y > 0) -> both positive.

title: Difference between = and ==
question_text: In programming, what is the difference between = and ==?
explanation: = assigns a value; == compares values. Using = in an if condition is a common bug.

title: Not equal symbol
question_text: Which symbol means 'not equal to' in most languages?
explanation: != is standard. Example: if (answer != 42) -> not correct.

title: Increment operator
question_text: What does ++ do?
explanation: ++ increments by 1. x++ is same as x = x + 1.

title: Backslash usage
question_text: What is the primary use of backslash (\) in programming strings?
explanation: Backslash escapes special characters. Examples: \n (newline), \t (tab), \\ (literal backslash).

title: Less than or equal
question_text: Which symbol means 'less than or equal to'?
explanation: <= is less than or equal. Example: if (score <= 100) -> valid.

title: Square root in code
question_text: How do you calculate the square root of 16 in most programming languages?
explanation: Most languages provide a sqrt() function: Math.sqrt(16) in JavaScript/Java, math.sqrt(16) in Python.

title: Logical OR
question_text: Which operator returns true if at least one condition is true?
explanation: || is logical OR. Example: if (isWeekend || isHoliday) -> day off.

title: Assignment vs Comparison
question_text: What is the correct way to check if x is equal to 5?
explanation: x == 5 compares. x = 5 would assign 5 to x, which is usually a bug.

title: Pi constant
question_text: What is the approximate value of π?
explanation: π ≈ 3.14159. Used for circles: circumference = 2πr.

title: Modulo use case
question_text: Which problem can modulo (%) solve?
opt2: Check if a number is even or odd
explanation: n % 2 == 0 is even, n % 2 == 1 is odd. Also used for cyclic indices.

title: Difference between / and \
question_text: What is the difference between forward slash (/) and backslash (\) in programming?
opt4: / is path separator on Windows, \ is on Linux
explanation: / is division (and path on Linux/macOS). \ is escape (and Windows path).

title: Logical NOT
question_text: What does ! (exclamation) do?
explanation: ! reverses boolean: !true = false, !false = true.

title: Double quote name
question_text: What is the name of the symbol " ?
explanation: It is called 'double quote' or 'quotation mark'.

title: Underscore name
question_text: What is the name of the symbol _ ?
opt4: Underline (both b and d are correct, but most common is underscore)
explanation: Underscore (or underline). Used in snake_case variable names.

title: At sign usage
question_text: In Python, what is the @ symbol used for?
explanation: @ is used for decorators in Python. Example: @staticmethod

title: Hash symbol name
question_text: What is the most common name for # in programming?
explanation: Most programmers say 'hash'. Example: # comment in Python.

title: Dollar sign usage
question_text: In PHP, what does $ indicate?
explanation: $ precedes variable names in PHP: $name = 'John';

title: Dot in code
question_text: What does the dot (.) do in JavaScript?
explanation: Dot is used for decimal numbers (3.14) and property access (user.name). In some languages, + is for concatenation.

title: Semicolon purpose
question_text: What is the primary purpose of semicolon (;) in C/Java?
explanation: Semicolon terminates statements. Example: x = 5;

title: Colon in ternary
question_text: In the ternary operator `condition ? a : b`, what does the colon mean?
opt3: Separator between true and false values
explanation: Colon separates the true value from the false value. Read as 'colon'.

title: Question mark in optional chaining
question_text: In JavaScript, what does `?.` mean?
explanation: `?.` is optional chaining: it returns undefined if the left side is null/undefined.

title: Ellipsis name
question_text: What is the name of `...` in JavaScript?
explanation: Most common names: 'spread operator', 'rest operator', or 'dot dot dot'.

title: Backtick usage
question_text: In JavaScript, what are backticks `` ` `` used for?
explanation: Backticks allow template literals: `${variable}` inside.

title: Escape sequence for newline
question_text: How do you write a newline character in a string?
explanation: Backslash n: \n. Read as 'backslash n'.

title: Hyphen vs minus
question_text: In a CSS class name `user-profile`, how do you read the '-' ?
opt4: Dash (both b and c are accepted)
example1: When inside an identifier, it is called 'hyphen' or 'dash', not 'minus'.

title: Address-of operator (& in C++)
definition: In C++, & before a variable means 'address of'. It returns the memory address of the variable. Also used for reference types (int& ref = x).
example3: int x = 5; int* ptr = &x; (ptr gets address of x)
tip: Do not confuse with bitwise AND (x & y)
description: Read as 'address of' when before a variable: &x = 'address of x'.

title: Pipe (|) in shell
definition: In Unix/Linux shell, the pipe symbol | sends the output of one command as input to another. Also used as bitwise OR (single pipe) and logical OR (double pipe ||).
example3: ls -l | grep '.txt' -> 'list long pipe grep dot txt'
tip: In code: let flags = FLAG1 | FLAG2 (bitwise OR)
description: When reading shell commands, say 'pipe' for |. Example: 'ls pipe grep'.

title: Not equal in SQL (<>)
definition: In SQL, <> means 'not equal to'. Some languages also use <> for inequality (e.g., Basic, Pascal). Most C-style languages use !=.
example2: SQL: SELECT * FROM users WHERE age <> 18;
image_url: Read as 'less than greater than' or 'not equal to'. But in SQL, say 'not equal'.

title: Bitwise shift left (<<) and right (>>)
definition: Shift all bits left (<<) or right (>>). Left shift multiplies by 2 for each position; right shift divides (integer division). Also used in C++ for stream insertion/extraction.
image_url: Left shift by n is multiply by 2^n. Right shift by n is divide by 2^n (floor).

title: Null coalescing operator (??)
definition: Returns the left-hand side if it is not null, otherwise returns the right-hand side. Available in C#, JavaScript, PHP, and others.
usage1: Difference from ||: ?? only checks null/undefined, not falsy values.
image_url: Read as 'question mark question mark'. In JavaScript, ?? is 'nullish coalescing'.

title: Optional chaining (?.)
definition: Allows reading a property deep inside an object without checking if each reference is valid. If any part is null/undefined, the expression returns undefined.
example4: Equivalent to: if (user && user.address) city = user.address.city; else city = undefined;
image_url: Read as 'question mark dot'. Example: 'user question mark dot address question mark dot city'.

title: Null coalescing assignment (??=)
definition: Assigns the right-hand side to the left-hand side only if the left-hand side is null or undefined. Available in JavaScript, C#, etc.
example2: JavaScript: x ??= 10; (assigns 10 only if x is null or undefined)
example4: Equivalent to: if (x === null || x === undefined) x = 10;
image_url: Read as 'question mark question mark equals'. Say 'nullish coalescing assignment' in explanation.

title: Scope resolution operator (::)
definition: In C++, :: is used to access namespaces, static members, and to define methods outside the class. Also used in PHP for static methods.
image_url: Read as 'double colon' or 'scope resolution'.

title: Windows line ending (\r\n)
definition: Two-character sequence: carriage return (\r) followed by newline (\n). Used as line terminator in Windows text files. Read as 'backslash r backslash n'.
usage1: Comparing files: different line endings cause false differences
image_url: When reading, say 'backslash r backslash n' or 'Windows line ending'.

title: Address-of operator in C++
question_text: What does & mean when placed before a variable in C++ (e.g., &x)?
explanation: & before variable gives memory address. Example: int* ptr = &x;

title: Pipe in shell
question_text: In Unix/Linux shell, what does the | symbol do?
opt2: Send output of left command as input to right command
explanation: Pipe connects commands: left | right. Example: ls | grep txt

title: SQL not equal
question_text: In SQL, which symbol means 'not equal to'?
explanation: SQL uses <> for not equal. Example: WHERE age <> 18

title: Left shift effect
question_text: What does 1 << 3 evaluate to in most languages?
explanation: 1 << 3 shifts bits left by 3 positions: binary 0001 becomes 1000 = 8.

title: Null coalescing operator
question_text: What does the ?? operator do in JavaScript?
opt2: Nullish coalescing: returns right side if left is null/undefined
explanation: ?? returns left if not null/undefined, otherwise right. Different from ||.

title: Optional chaining
question_text: What does `user?.address?.city` do in JavaScript?
opt1: Crashes if user or address is null
opt2: Safely accesses city, returns undefined if any part is null
explanation: Optional chaining (?.) stops and returns undefined if any property is null/undefined.

title: Null coalescing assignment
question_text: What does `x ??= 10` do?
opt2: Assigns 10 only if x is null or undefined
explanation: ??= assigns right side only if left is null/undefined.

title: Scope resolution operator
question_text: In C++, what is :: used for?
opt2: Double colon for namespace, static members, and method definition
explanation: Example: std::cout (std namespace) or MyClass::myMethod

title: Windows line ending
question_text: What characters represent a line ending in Windows text files?
explanation: Windows uses carriage return + newline: \r\n
